coredata.dc:title: Polypeptide uploaded efficient nanophotosensitizers to overcome photodynamic resistance for enhanced anticancer therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yanjing
Zhou, Ronghui
Xiao, Dexuan
Shi, Sirong
Peng, Shuanglin
Wu, Shihong
Wu, Peng
Lin, Yunfeng
coredata.dc:description: Being either minimally invasive or non-invasive, photodynamic therapy (PDT) has garnered much attention in treatment of superficial tumors. However, repeated irradiation of tumors may lead to PDT resistance, mediated by cell survival pathways, and may result in decreased PDT efficiency and increased emotional burden of patients. Herein, we describe a high-performance nanosystem based on N,S-codoped graphene quantum dots (GQDs), established to overcome PDT resistance in cancer via uploading cyclic (RGDfC) peptide. The as-prepared cRGD-GQDs possessed good biocompatibility, high singlet oxygen quantum yield (ΦΔ = 0.95), and excellent pH stability. Owing to the specific binding ability between c(RGDfC) and integrin αvβ3, the cRGD-GQDs could successfully aggregate in tumor cells and continuously generate high levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Notably, some PDT resistance-related factors, such as antioxidant proteins (Nrf2, HO-1, and NQO-1) and the pump protein ABCG2, were maintained at a low level after repetitive PDT treatment mediated by cRGD-GQDs. However, significant upregulation of these factors can be determined after repetitive PDT treatment with free GQDs. Thus, the cRGD-GQDs exhibited powerful antitumor PDT effects both in vitro and in vivo by inhibiting the survival pathways of cancer cells. In conclusion, this study presents a potential appealing clinical strategy in overcoming the acquired PDT resistance and has far-reaching significance for the future PDT therapies.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective uptake and cytotoxicity in vitro
Mechanism of GQDs mediated PDT resistance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of cRGD-GQDs. (A) Zeta potential of GQDs and cRGD-GQDs; (B) Spectra of free GQDs, cRGD, and cRGD-GQDs between 200 and 700 nm; (C) Hydrodynamic size of GQDs and cRGD-GQDs detected by dynamic light scattering; (D) UV − vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 420 nm) of cRGD-GQDs; (E) Fluorescence intensity of cRGD-GQDs in pH ranging from 3.66 to 9; (F) Characterized 1O2 phosphorescence emissions of RB, Ce6 and cRGD-GQDs at 1275 nm.

Morphology and composition of cRGD-GQDs. (A) TEM image of cRGD-GQDs. Scale bars, 50 nm; (B) HR-TEM image of cRGD-GQDs. Scale bars, 5 nm; (C) XPS survey spectrum of the GQDs and cRGD-GQDs; (D) EDS element mapping and patterns of the prepared nano-particles. Scale bars: 200 nm.

Selective uptake and cytotoxicity in vitro. (A, B) The uptake of GQDs and cRGD-GQDs by A375 and L929 cell lines analyzed by flow cytometry; (C, D) The uptake of GQDs and cRGD-GQDs by A375 and L929 cells analyzed by confocal laser scanning microscope. Scale bars: 40 μm; (E, F) Comparison of the PDT performance of GQDs with cRGD-GQDs in A375 and L929 cells. Data are performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and presented as mean ± SD (n ≥ 3). Statistical analysis: * vs control, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01; #P < 0.05, ##P < 0.01.

Establishment of photodynamic resistant cell line. (A) Cell viability after repeated GQD-mediated PDT treatment; Data are performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and presented as mean ± SD (n ≥ 3). Statistical analysis: * compare with the control cells followed by the same number of PDT treatments, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01; # compare with cells incubated with the same concentration of GQDs followed with one PDT treatment, #P < 0.05, ##P < 0.01; Anti x2: cells after two PDT treatments; Anti x4: cells after four PDT treatments; Anti x6: cells after six PDT treatments; Anti x8: cells after eight PDT treatments; (B) Intracellular GQDs in cells after incubation and repeated PDT treatment detected by flow cytometry; (C) Fluorescent images of ROS generated during repeated GQDs-mediated PDT with a ROS probe, DCFH-DA. Scale bars: 50 μm.

cRGD-GQDs overcome GQDs mediated PDT resistance. (A) Cell viability of resistant cell lines after 50 nM GQDs or cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT. Data are performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and presented as mean ± SD (n ≥ 3). Statistical analysis: * compare with the control cells followed by cells given the same number of PDT treatments, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01; #P < 0.05, ##P < 0.01; &P < 0.05, &&P < 0.01; (B) Intracellular cRGD-GQDs in cells detected by flow cytometry after incubation and repeated irradiations, PDT x2 means two irradiations after incubation with cRGD-GQDs; (C) Detection of ROS during cRGD@GQD-mediated repetitive PDT with a ROS probe, DCFH-DA; nuclei were labeled by Hoechst33342. Scale bars: 50 μm.

Mechanism of photodynamic resistance. (A) Western bolt images of the expression of Nrf2, HO-1, and ABCG2 proteins; (B) Quantitative analysis of Nrf2, HO-1, and ABCG2 proteins. Statistical significance was determined by students t-test. Data are performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and presented as mean ± SD (n ≥ 3). Statistical analysis: *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01; (C) Quantitative RT-PCR analysis of the expression of Nrf2, ABCG2, HO-1, NQO-1 and HIF-1α during the appearance of PDT resistance. Data are performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and presented as mean ± SD (n ≥ 3). Statistical analysis: *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01; (D) Western blot images showing changes in PI3K/Akt signaling proteins; (E) Quantitative analysis of changes in PI3K/Akt signaling proteins. Data are performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and presented as mean ± SD (n ≥ 3). Statistical analysis: *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

In vivo PDT efficiency of GQDs. (A) Relative tumor volume (V/V0) upon different treatments. Data are performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and presented as mean ± SD (n ≥ 3). Statistical analysis: * comparation between the control group and cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT group, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01; # comparation between the control group and GQD-mediated PDT group, #P < 0.05, ##P < 0.01; & comparation between the control group and GQD-mediated PDT group, &P < 0.05, &&P < 0.01; (B) Weight of mice with or without PDT. Data are performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and presented as mean ± SD (n ≥ 3). Statistical analysis: *P < 0.05; (C) Photographs of mice after PDT on the 1st, 5th and 15th day. (D) H&E staining of tumor tissues in different treatment groups. Scale bars: 400 μm.
Summary on the pleiotropic mechanism of cRGD-GQDs-mediated enhanced PDT.

head.ce:title: Polypeptide uploaded efficient nanophotosensitizers to overcome photodynamic resistance for enhanced anticancer therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Being either minimally invasive or non-invasive, photodynamic therapy (PDT) has garnered much attention in treatment of superficial tumors. However, repeated irradiation of tumors may lead to PDT resistance, mediated by cell survival pathways, and may result in decreased PDT efficiency and increased emotional burden of patients. Herein, we describe a high-performance nanosystem based on N,S-codoped graphene quantum dots (GQDs), established to overcome PDT resistance in cancer via uploading cyclic (RGDfC) peptide. The as-prepared cRGD-GQDs possessed good biocompatibility, high singlet oxygen quantum yield (ΦΔ = 0.95), and excellent pH stability. Owing to the specific binding ability between c(RGDfC) and integrin αvβ3, the cRGD-GQDs could successfully aggregate in tumor cells and continuously generate high levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Notably, some PDT resistance-related factors, such as antioxidant proteins (Nrf2, HO-1, and NQO-1) and the pump protein ABCG2, were maintained at a low level after repetitive PDT treatment mediated by cRGD-GQDs. However, significant upregulation of these factors can be determined after repetitive PDT treatment with free GQDs. Thus, the cRGD-GQDs exhibited powerful antitumor PDT effects both in vitro and in vivo by inhibiting the survival pathways of cancer cells. In conclusion, this study presents a potential appealing clinical strategy in overcoming the acquired PDT resistance and has far-reaching significance for the future PDT therapies.

ce:section.ce:para: PDT is an emerging cancer treatment that entails induction of ROS via administration of photosensitizers (PSs) and local irradiation, which may subsequently result in cancer cell death [1–3]. PDT has been approved for clinical application, and widely used in treating tumors in skin, oral mucosa, and other superficial areas due to the minimally invasive treatment modality with low side effects [4–6]. However, numerous clinical studies have demonstrated the development of resistance after repeated PDT treatment with 5-ALA, photofrin, and Nile Blue A, resulting in decreased PDT efficiency [7–9]. The emergence of PDT resistance can be attributed to insufficient ROS generation, which activates a series of survival pathways [10]. As a result, a significant percentage of cells recover from PDT damage with changes in gene mutations, protein expression, and cell behavior, which then directly affect the response to ongoing treatment and the efficacy of anticancer therapy. For example, researchers have confirmed PDT resistance via elevated expression of antioxidant proteins and pump proteins through p38MAPK and phosphatidylinositol-3-kinases/protein-serine-threonine kinase (PI3K) pathways. The increased antioxidant proteins and pump proteins could reduce intracellular PS concentration and ROS generation, resulting in inadequate PDT efficiency [11–13]. Meanwhile, hypoxia and antitumor immune response can also help cancer cells to cope with PDT-induced cell damage [10]. Therefore, the quality of PDT can be significantly improved if the inhibition strategies for these survival pathways are known.

To overcome this issue, recent studies have utilized inhibitors of PDT resistance-related proteins, such as those of the Nrf2-HO-1 or Nrf2-ABCG2 signaling axes, or explored oxygen catalysts to enhance the efficacy of PDT by increasing ROS [14–21]. For example, a tyrosine kinase inhibitor can enhance PDT efficiency through blocking the function of pump proteins [22]. Shi et al. combined an Fe3+ and Bcl-2 inhibitor to elevate intracellular ROS concentration and overcome PDT resistance [23]. However, it was limited by the complicated administration of inhibitors as well as the relatively low ROS generation efficiency of organic PSs. At present, various carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon dots (CDs), GQDs, fullerenes, and carbon nitride, have been explored for PDT owing to the merits of high photostability and good biocompatibility [24]. Particularly, red emissive N,S-codoped GQDs that developed by Ge et al. possessed a broad absorption band with an ultrahigh singlet oxygen yield of 1.3 [25], and the 1O2 generation ability that is greater than carbon dots (ΦΔ = 0.82)[26], C60 (ΦΔ = 0.52) [27], resulting in an excellent performance in PDT. Additionally, our previous study demonstrated that GQDs were highly efficient in PDT by activating the intrinsic apoptotic pathway via the synergistic effect of p38/JNK and PI3K/Akt signaling pathways, promoted by its high singlet oxygen yield. Thus, GQD-mediated PDT is well suited for treatment of superficial tumors, as the tumors are penetrated by the light source more easily. Unfortunately, we found that GQDs could also induce PDT resistance after repeated irradiation. Therefore, suppressing GQD-induced PDT resistance is necessary.

Integrin αvβ3 is highly expressed in many cancers, such as malignant melanoma. It is a cell-adhesion molecule, which is associated with tumor growth, metastasis, and tumor vascularization but is rarely expressed on normal cells. Cyclic (RGDfC) (c(RGDfC)) is a ligand to integrin αvβ3 [28], which is widely utilized in targeted imaging and anticancer therapy [29–32]. PDT has been successfully used in treating malignant melanoma, one of the most aggressive superficial tumors [33]. However, resistance to PDT has been reported in melanoma [34]. Therefore, a high-performance nanosystem was established based on N,S-codoped GQDs. GQDs were labelled with c(RGDfC) to improve nano-PS delivery and overcome PDT resistance in malignant melanoma (Scheme 1 ). Furthermore, the prepared cRGD-GQDs nanocrystals were evaluated for biocompatibility, singlet oxygen quantum yield, and pH stability. Our study will offer a potential strategy to overcome the acquired PDT resistance in superficial tumors and significance for future PDT therapies.

4-Bromobenzyl bromide, N, N-dimethyldodecylamine, and thiophene-3-boronic acid were purchased from Energy Chemical, China. Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) and Fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Gibco BRL. Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) were obtained from MedChemExpress, USA. Hoechest-33342 and 2′, 7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) were obtained from Beyondtime Bio-Tech, China. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm−1) was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q purification system and was used in all the experiments. All the chemical reagents used herein were of analytical grade.

GQDs: GQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of PT2 as previous described [25]. Briefly, PT2 was dispersed in 0.5 mM NaOH solution, and then ultrasonicated for 30 min. The mixture was then transferred into an autoclave and heated at 170 ℃ for 24 h, cooled down to room temperature and purified via filtering through 0.22-µm membranes and further dialyzed against distilled water.

cRGD-GQDs: 1 mmol of c(RGDfC) powder was dispersed in 100 µL double distilled water (ddH2O), which was then added to the GQD solution (2 µM) with stirring at 4 °C for 2 h. The final products were purified by centrifugation using a 10 K centrifugal filter device (Millipore, USA) at 8,000 × g for 6 min.

The UV absorbance of cRGD, GQDs, and cRGD-GQDs were detected by an ultra-micro spectrophotometer, NanoPhotometer N60 (Implen, Germany). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta potential analyses of aqueous cRGD, GQDs and cRGD-GQDs were performed with a laser light scattering system (Nano ZS, Malvern, England). UV-1750 (Shimadzu, Japan) and Lamda365 (Perkin-Elmer, USA) were utilized to characterize the absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of cRGD-GQDs. Characteristic phosphorescence of 1O2 at 1275 nm was observed from GQDs upon excitation at 420 nm by Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, JobinYvon). Fluorescence stability of cRGD-GQDs was detected by adding cRGD-GQDs into Na2HPO4/NaH2PO4 buffer solution with the pH ranging from 3.6 to 9 using Varioskan LUX (Thermo Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) and mapping of cRGD-GQDs were performed using a transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV (JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was performed using the AXIS Ultra DLD (Kratos, UK) to analyze the valent state of cRGD-GQDs.

A375, and L929 cells were incubated with 25 nM GQDs or cRGD-GQDs overnight. Flow cytometry was performed with 106 cells collected in 500 µL PBS using Attune NxT Flow Cytometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). For fluorescence images, cells were fixed with paraformaldehyde and the cytoskeleton were stained with phalloidin. Finally, the samples were observed using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), AIR-MP (Nikon, Japan).

A375, and L929 cells were incubated with GQDs or cRGD-GQDs at 37 °C in 5% CO2 overnight. After removing the PSs, cells were irradiated using an ultraviolet light-emitting diode (LED) (λ = 420 nm) with an intensity of 80 mW·cm−2 for 5 min. Cell viability was then determined by cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8). Optical densities (OD) were then measured at 450 nm using Varioskan LUX (Thermo Scientific, USA). The experiments were performed 3 times.

To evaluate the establishment of the anti-PDT cell line, A375 were incubated with 50 nM GQDs followed by repeated irradiation every two hours with an intensity of 80 mW·cm−2 for 2 min. After two, four, six and eight irradiations, cells were rinsed with PBS and cultured in fresh medium for 24 h. The cells that survived were incubated in different concentration of GQDs (0–100 nM) for 12 h, and irradiated with an intensity of 80 mW·cm−2 for 5 min after removing PSs and replacing the media. Cell viability was detected using CCK-8 assay kit, and the fold resistance index was calculated as follows:
Fold resistance = Resistant cell line IC(50)/parental cell line IC(50).

A375 cells were seeded in 12-well plates. After different treatments, the cells were then washed with PBS twice and stained with DCFH-DA to analyze ROS generation and Hoechst33342 was used as a nuclear stain. The fluorescence of DCFH-DA was immediately observed using DFC7000 T fluorescent inverted microscope (Leica, Germany).

2 × 106 cells were seeded in a 6-well plate, and treated with different times PDT. The cells were then washed with ice-cold PBS for 3 times, and lysed in lysis buffer to harvest total proteins. Protein concentrations were measured by NanoPhotometer N60 (Implen, Germany). The protein samples were separated on 6% or 10% (v/v) SDS polyacrylamide gels depending on the molecular weight and transferred into polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membranes. The membranes were blocked with 5% dehydrated milk or bovine serum albumin, and then incubated with the following primary antibodies: anti-GAPDH (1:1000, Cell Signaling Technology), anti-Bax (1:1000, Abcam), anti-Bcl-2 (1:1000, Abcam), anti-caspase-3 (1:1000, Abcam), anti-PI3K p85α (1:1000, Abcam), anti-PI3K p110β (1:1000, Abcam), anti-Akt (1:1000, Cell Signaling Technology), anti-Akt (phospho Ser473) (1:1000, Cell Signaling Technology), anti-Nrf2 (1:1000, Abcam), anti-ABCG2 (1:1000, Abcam), and anti-HO-1 (1:1000, Abcam). Membranes were then washed thrice in TBST and incubated with secondary antibodies (anti-rabbit or anti-mouse respectively) for 1 h at room temperature. Chemiluminescence was developed using ECL reagents.

Total RNA of all samples was extracted via Trizol extraction method. After resuspension in the RNase-free water, total mRNA was quantified using a spectrophotometer and reverse transcribed into cDNA using a cDNA synthesis kit (Takara Bio, China). The expression of target mRNAs (Table S1) in each treatment group was normalized to GAPDH and evaluated. RT-PCR was performed using SYBR® Green I PCR master mix by ABI QuantStudio6 Q6 Real-Time PCR System (Thermo Scientific, USA).

Animal experiments were approved by the China Committee for Research and Animal Ethics in compliance with the laws on experimental animals. Specific pathogen-free female BALB/c nude mice, 3–4 weeks of age, were purchased from GemPharmatech Co. Ltd, and bred in a SPF grade environment. A malignant melanoma model was established by injecting A375 cells (1 × 106 cells in 100 µL) subcutaneously into the left anterior armpit, and tumors were allowed to grow to approximately 100 mm3. These mice were separated into 5 groups with 6 mice per group. Mice in the control group were injected with normal saline. For the cRGD-GQDs group, mice were injected with cRGD-GQDs (4 mg/kg), and mice in the irradiation group were irradiated for 15 min with 450 nm laser at a power density of 80 mW·cm−2. In the GQDs or cRGD-GQDs mediated PDT group, mice were intravenously injected with GQDs or cRGD-GQDs (4 mg/kg) and then irradiated for 15 min with 450 nm laser at a power density of 300 mW. During treatment, mice were anesthetized with 2.5% isoflurane. Sliding calipers were employed to measure the greatest longitudinal diameter (length) and the greatest transverse diameter (width) of each tumor. Tumor volume (V) was calculated using length × width2 × 0.5, and the relative tumor volumes were calculated for each mouse as V/V0 (V0 was the tumor volume when the treatment was initiated). Pictures of mice during therapy were taken using a Huawei P20 Pro. Mice were sacrificed after 15 days after initiation of treatment, the tumors and important organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) were harvest and subjected to H&E staining.

All experiments were performed more than thrice, and the experimental data were performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and analyzed via SPSS 16.0. P values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

cRGD-GQDs were synthesized utilizing the electrostatic attraction between GQDs and c(RGDfC). As shown in Fig. 1 A, GQDs possessed positive potential, while the surface of cRGD-GQDs was negatively charged, and the change of zeta potential confirmed the formation of electrovalent bond between GQDs and c(RGDfC). Moreover, the characteristic absorption peak of both cRGD (~260 nm) and GQDs (~420 nm) could be identified from the absorption of UV–Vis characterization of the prepared nanoparticles, which further indicated the successful synthesis of cRGD-GQDs (Fig. 1B), and according to UV–Vis result, the loading efficiency of cRGD was about 61.6%. Furthermore, the slightly increased hydrodynamic size of the nanoparticle also suggested the successful preparation of cRGD-GQDs (Fig. 1C).

Optical properties were also analyzed, as shown in Fig. 1D. The UV–vis absorption of cRGD-GQDs spans from 400 to 600 nm, with red fluorescence emission at ~630 nm (λex = 420 nm), indicating a large Stokes shift that favors biological applications [35]. The stability of cRGD-GQDs in physiological conditions was detected by DLS and fluorescence intensity, and results show that cRGD-GQDs could remain relatively stable diameter in both 10% FBS or cell lysate at least for 24 h (Fig. S1). Since lysosomes usually possess the lowest pH (~5) in cells, the pH stability of PSs is important in ensuring therapeutic efficiency. The fluorescence of cRGD-GQDs was detected at various pH ranging from 3.66 to 9, and Fig. 1E shows that cRGD-GQDs maintained stable fluorescence intensity even in acidic environments, indicating the excellent pH stability of the nano-particles. The characteristic phosphorescence of 1O2 at 1275 nm was also observed from cRGD-GQDs upon excitation at 420 nm (Fig. 1F) and using Rose Bengal (RB) as the standard (ΦΔ = 0.75), the singlet oxygen quantum yield (QY) of cRGD-GQDs was determined to be ~0.95. For the classic photosensitizer Ce6, the 1O2 QY was ~0.27, much lower than that of cRGD-GQDs.

The morphology of the prepared nanoparticles was determined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM). As presented in Fig. 2 A, the size of cRGD-GQDs ranged from 20 to 40 nm, coinciding with the hydrodynamic size detected by DLS. Moreover, HR-TEM revealed the crystallinity of the synthesized nanoparticles, and the interplanar distance was about 0.22 nm (Fig. 2B). Furthermore, the composition of cRGD-GQDs was further analyzed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and the result, as indicated in Fig. 2C, revealed the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S) and oxygen (O) in both cRGD-GQDs and pure GQDs spectroscopy. Moreover, for cRGD-GQDs, the O1 signal was increased in height, indicating successful loading of cRGD onto the nanoparticle, as the carboxyl groups are present in cRGD. Similarly, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) data (Fig. 2D) also revealed the distributions of C, N, S and O in cRGD-GQDs, were consistent with XPS results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective uptake and cytotoxicity in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Integrin αvβ3 is highly expressed on many cancer cells and is also a target for tumor imaging and anticancer therapy [36]. c(RGDfC) can specifically bind to integrin αvβ3 and is widely utilized in targeting tumors. Human malignant melanoma cell line A375, which expresses a high level of integrin αvβ3, was utilized to verify the selective uptake of cRGD-GQDs. L929 (mouse fibroblast cell line), which rarely expressed integrin αvβ3 protein, was selected as the negative control. Selective uptake was evaluated by incubating cells with free GQDs or cRGD-GQDs overnight and then analyzed via flow cytometry and confocal laser scanning microscope. As shown in Fig. 3 A, B, A375 cells incubated with cRGD-GQDs showed higher fluorescence intensity as compared to those incubated with GQDs, while L929 incubated with GQDs showed stronger fluorescence than cells incubated with cRGD-GQDs, indicating that cRGD-GQDs could target integrin αvβ3 positive cancer cells and demonstrated decreased uptake in normal cells. Furthermore, fluorescence imaging also confirmed the cancer cell specific intake of cRGD-GQDs (Fig. 3C, D). A plausible explanation for the selective uptake of cRGD-GQDs is that GQDs possessed positive surface charge, which is favorable for cell uptake; it could actively enter many kinds of cells. However, the negatively charge on the surface of cRGD-GQDs was not conducive to cellular endocytosis, and internalization of cRGD-GQDs towards integrin αvβ3 positive cancer cells was aided by the binding of the polypeptide with the surface receptor. The type of endocytosis involved in cRGD-GQDs uptake was also detected by flow cytometry after incubating cells with inhibitors for the different endocytosis pathways separately. The results demonstrated that cRGD-GQDs were endocytosed by cancer cells primarily via dynamin-GTPase/clathrin-mediated endocytosis (Fig. S2). Further investigation revealed that cRGD-GQDs were primarily localized in mitochondria and lysosomes after 12 h incubation (Fig. S3). Previous studies have demonstrated that concomitant mitochondrial or lysosomal damage bolstered PDT induced cell death [37,38]. As cRGD-GQDs were primarily localized in the lysosome and mitochondria, they may demonstrate high efficacy in eradicating cancer cells.

To further verify the selectivity of cRGD-GQDs to cancer cells, in vitro cytotoxicity was measured by CCK-8 assay. A375 and L929 cells were incubated with GQDs or cRGD-GQDs overnight, and then irradiated with an intensity of 25 J·cm−2 for 5 min. As shown in Fig. 3E, cancer cell viability was about 40% after 25 nM cRGD-GQDs mediated PDT, whereas a cell viability of approximately 80% was observed for the same concentration of GQDs treatment. cRGD-GQDs induced more cancer cell death compared with GQDs, also indicating the selectivity of cRGD-GQDs for cancer cells. In contrast, L929 cells could survive from cRGD-GQDs mediated PDT due to reduced uptake of cRGD-GQDs in normal cells (Fig. 3F). The significant differences in cytotoxicity between the two particles on different cells were ascribed to the greater internalization of cRGD-GQDs to ανβ3 integrin receptor-positive cancer cells. Our previous study revealed that GQDs mediated PDT induced cell death primarily through mitochondria mediated apoptosis [39]. cRGD-GQDs mediated PDT, like GQDs mediated PDT, also induced cancer cell apoptosis via disturbance of Bcl-2 family proteins and caspase cascades through PI3K/Akt signaling pathway (Fig. S4).

Although PDT has been demonstrated to be efficacious in treating cancer, development of PDT resistance greatly reduces its efficacy. PDT resistance has been reported in many organic PSs; however, resistance to nano-PSs has rarely been reported. According to Alicia et al. [40], the acquired resistance may be attributed to repeated treatment, so we postulated that repeated administration of GQD-mediated PDT may induce resistance. To evaluate the establishment of the anti-PDT cell line, cells were incubated with 50 nM GQDs followed by two, four, six or eight irradiations with an intensity of 10 J·cm−2 for 2 min, and then cell viability of the different cell lines was detected by CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 4 A, 70% of A375 cells were eradicated after 50 nM GQD-mediated PDT after the first irradiation. However, only about 40% of cells died in the groups treated 6 or 8 times, emphasizing the possible occurrence of PDT resistance. To calculate fold resistance index, the half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) was first calculated for all cell lines. IC50 of normal A375 cells was about 36.12 nM, and after eight repetitions of PDT treatment, the index reached about 80.78 nM. As shown in Fig. 4A, the fold resistance index was greater than 1.5 after two consecutive PDT treatments and increased with incremental irradiation times, suggesting that cells could be considered resistant after consecutive free GQD-mediated PDT.

The intracellular concentration of GQDs after different treatments was also detected using flow cytometry. As displayed in Fig. 4B, most A375 cells could endocytose GQDs, but almost no free GQDs were left in the cells after two irradiations, suggesting that GQDs were eliminated after repeated PDT treatment. Additionally, the generation of ROS was also assessed. GQDs and irradiation together exhibited strong ROS fluorescence in cells, while the intensity of green signal was significantly lower after repeated irradiations and almost no ROS was displayed after six irradiations (Fig. 4C). These results demonstrated that repeated GQD-mediated PDT could induce PDT resistance in cancer cells, with the PS being pumped out along with attenuation of ROS generation. GQDs were highly efficient nano-PS; however, the reappearance of resistance principally limited the anticancer therapies’ effectiveness.

To explore whether cRGD-GQDs could overcome GQD-mediated PDT resistance, the acquired resistant cancer cell lines were treated with the same concentration of GQD- or cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT. CCK-8 analysis showed that the resistant A375 cells demonstrated approximately 40% cell survival after 50 nM GQDs mediated PDT treatments, but only 8% cells could survive after 50 nM cRGD-GQDs mediated PDT (Fig. 5 A). Similar to other resistant cell lines, resistant A375 showed better sensitivity to cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT as compared to the same concentration of GQD-mediated PDT, demonstrating that cRGD-GQDs may overcome GQD-mediated PDT resistance and maintain high efficacy in killing cancer cells. Furthermore, the intracellular cRGD-GQDs after different treatments were also detected using flow cytometry. As shown in Fig. 5B, cells after incubation with cRGD-GQDs showed the greatest uptake of the nanoparticles, and maintained the intracellular accumulation of cRGD-GQDs after repeated PDT treatments. The results indicated that repeated cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT did not induce the pumping out of this nanoparticle and may not induce PDT resistance. Furthermore, generation of ROS was detected via fluorescent inverted microscopy. We surprisingly observed that intracellular ROS generation was almost equal after both the first and eighth cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT treatments (Fig. 5C). These results demonstrated that cRGD-GQDs could be maintained in cancer cells even during repeated PDT treatments and produced constant ROS, and could overcome the PDT resistance to mediate highly efficient anticancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of GQDs mediated PDT resistance
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of PDT resistance was primarily investigated by analyzing the expressions of antioxidant proteins and pumping proteins after cells were treated with free GQDs or cRGD-GQDs followed by repetitive irradiation. Nrf2 is a transcription factor that regulates expression of several antioxidant proteins and drugs transporters. Furthermore, previous studies have reported that that Nrf2 signaling was upregulated after exposure to chemotherapeutic drugs or PDT therapy, conferring resistance to these therapies [41–43]. Herein, we monitored its expression after repeated GQD- and cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT therapy in A375 cells, to investigate the mechanism of GQD-mediated PDT resistance. As speculated, the expression of Nrf2 protein in A375 cells treated with GQDs followed by repetitive irradiations was higher compared to the control group (Fig. 6 A, B). This demonstrated that activated stress response of cancer cells could be triggered by intracellular oxidative imbalance. These results demonstrated that PDT-resistant cancer cells had developed the ability of reducing degree of oxidation by upregulating Nrf2. Nevertheless, as displayed in Fig. 6A and B, the expression of Nrf2 in cancer cells treated with cRGD-GQDs was found remain low after double irradiation and began to decrease with increasing irradiation treatment times. The transcription level of Nrf2 was consistent with protein variation, with significant increase in the GQD-treated group and remarkable decrease in cRGD@GQD-treated group (Fig. 6C). These results reasonably explained the depletion of ROS after free GQD-mediated PDT, while ROS was continuously generated in cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT group. This indicated that PDT resistance induced by repetitive GQD-mediated PDT could be overcome by cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT via Nrf2 depended pathway.

ATP-binding cassette, subfamily G, member 2 (ABCG2) has drawn much attention in development of resistance to antitumor therapy. ABCG2, expressed on the membrane of specific types of cells, is responsible for binding and transporting a wide range of substrates within and out of cells. It plays a vital role in maintaining homeostasis by preventing the accumulation of exogenous or endogenous chemicals, which may be detrimental to cell vitality or development [44,45]. Various tumor cells also express ABCG2, which is regulated by Nrf2, functions as a multidrug resistance protein, causing the efflux of therapeutic agents, such as some chemotherapy drugs and organic photosensitizers. It decreases their intracellular concentration, leading to a strong resistance to chemotherapy or PDT, thus decreasing clinical response [46–48]. It is well established that ABCG2 may contribute to PDT resistance mediated by PSs, such as benzoporphyrin derivative (BPD), chlorin-based drugs, aminolevulinic acid (ALA)-induced protoporphyrin IX (PpIX), and 2-(1-hexyloxyethyl)-2-devinyl pyropheophorbide-a (HPPH) [46,49]. As presented in Fig. 6A and B, the expression of ABCG2 protein was found to be upregulated in cells treated with GQD-mediated PDT twice, and increases with increasing irradiation times. Thus suggests that GQDs may be pumped out of cancer cells by ABCG2. The upregulation of ABCG2 was also an evidence for the low intracellular accumulation of GQDs during repeated GQD-mediated PDT (Fig. 4B). In contrast, the cRGD-GQDs treated group maintained a low level of ABCG2 protein during six irradiations compared with the PDT-resistant cells. Moreover, the transcription level of ABCG2 transporter was also detected. The mRNA level of ABCG2 displayed significant increase after incubation with GQDs followed by repeated irradiations, while cells treated with cRGD-GQDs followed by repetitive irradiations showed a decrease in ABCG2 mRNA levels (Fig. 6C). A reasonable explanation was that c(RGDfC) conjugating on GQDs might possess the ability of evading recognition by ABCG2 and decreased resistance of cells to cRGD-GQDs.

Nrf2 promotes the transcription of many antioxidant genes, such as heme oxygenase 1 (HO-1) and quinone dehydrogenase 1 (NQO-1), which protect cells from the damage by ROS [50,51]. HO-1 exerts a strong effect favoring tumor cell growth and resistance to therapy. It has been reported that increased expression of HO-1 could protect cancer cells from intracellular ROS damage [52,53]. Moreover, inhibition of HO-1 may increase the chemosensitivity of laryngeal squamous cell cancer to cisplatin [54]. Early studies also found that HO-1 expression could decrease the damage of photodynamic therapy to tumors, while inhibition of HO-1 could enhance the responsiveness of tumors to PDT [55,56]. Our results demonstrated that the expression of HO-1 protein was increased after repetitive GQD-mediated PDT (Fig. 6A, B). As observed from RT-PCR analysis, the transcription level of HO-1 and NQO-1 were significantly increased after GQDs incubation along with double irradiation and continued to increase after six irradiations (Fig. 6C). These results demonstrated that cancer cells may survive GQD-mediated PDT by upregulating expression of antioxidant proteins and attenuating ROS generation, resulting in photodynamic resistance. However, during the six repetitions of cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT, the level of HO-1 protein showed slight decrease after two irradiations and notable decrease after four irradiations (Fig. 6A, B). Furthermore, the mRNA level of HO-1 and NQO-1 presented relative decrease (Fig. 6C), indicating that cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT may evade PDT resistance via constant production of ROS.

Hypoxia-inducible factor-1α (HIF-1α), a transcription factor that senses oxygen homeostasis, is involved in hypoxia response, angiogenesis, hematopoiesis, and anaerobic energy metabolism. Previous clinical evidence proved that HIF expression is associated with PDT resistance [57,58]. PDT can induce the expression of HIF-1α due to photochemical consumption of oxygen, and upregulation of HIF-1α can induce the resistance of tumor cells to ALA-mediated PDT by favoring cell proliferation [13,59]. As shown in Fig. 6C, the expression of HIF-1α was significantly upregulated after GQDs incubation followed by repeated irradiation, illustrating cellular response to hypoxia and development of PDT resistance mediated by GQDs. In contrast, after two cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT treatments, the expression of HIF-1α significantly decreased and continued to remain low after further irradiation, suggesting that cRGD-GQDs could utilize oxygen more efficiently and overcome the PDT resistance mediated by GQDs.

To further investigate the mechanism of photodynamic resistance mediated by GQDs, we probed into the upstream signaling pathway. PI3K/Akt is one of the most activated signaling pathways in tumorigenesis and cancer resistance [60,61]. The signaling pathway is implicated in Nrf2 activation and ABCG2 expression, and inhibition of Akt can increase the drug efficacy by reducing the expression of ABCG2 in cancer cells [62,63]. We previously reported that GQDs mediated highly efficient PDT via a pleiotropic mechanism, including the inhibition of PI3K/Akt signaling pathway; however, its variation during reduplicative PDT was unclear. Here, we surprisingly observed that the inhibited PI3K/Akt protein expression levels were recovered after four GQD-mediated PDT treatments, and the signaling pathway showed remarkable increment after six irradiations (Fig. 6D, E), indicating that cells activated the survival mechanism, leading to PDT resistance. However, as expected, the PI3K/Akt pathway remained inhibited during repeated cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT, suggesting that cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT could sufficiently inhibit the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway during repetitive PDT treatments, leading to highly efficient PDT and overcoming PDT resistance mediated by free GQDs. In conclusion, the introduction of cRGD endows GQDs with the targeting ability, and it could significantly improve the intracellular accumulation of the nano-PS, and maintain the continuous generation of ROS. cRGD-GQDs mediated PDT could persistently suppress the cell survival signaling pathways, PI3K/Akt, on one way to promote cancer cell apoptosis directly, and on the other way to inhibit the expression of antioxidant proteins, leading to high levels of oxidative stress in cells. Moreover, the inhibition of PI3K/Akt could downregulate the expression of pump protein, leading to the evading recognition of cRGD-GQDs by ABCG2 and decreased the resistance of cells to cRGD-GQDs.

The in vivo PDT efficiency of GQDs and cRGD-GQDs was evaluated using female BALB/c nude mice with subcutaneous malignant melanoma xenografts. In vivo images show that at the same time point (2 h and 24 h) post injection of GQDs or cRGD-GQDs, tumor in cRGD-GQDs injection group displayed much stronger fluorescence than GQDs injection group, demonstrating the in vivo targeting ability of cRGD-GQDs (Fig. S5). GQDs and cRGD-GQDs were administered through intravenous injection overnight, then irradiated by laser. In vivo PDT was repeated every 48 h. As illustrated in Fig. 7 A, the relative tumor volume in both the GQD-mediated PDT group and cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT group showed extraordinary inhibition compared to the control group after double PDT, with no significant difference between the two groups. However, the tumors in the groups that received only cRGD-GQDs injection or irradiation showed no significant difference with that of the control group. As shown in Fig. 7C, tumors in the GQD- or cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT group were significantly smaller than those in the control group and there was no significant difference between the control group and groups that only received injection or irradiation. As PDT repetitions increased, the relative tumor volume in the GQD-mediated PDT group was significantly larger than that in the cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT group, and the gap between the two groups became increasingly apparent. On day 15, the tumors in the cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT group were visually smaller than those in the GQD-mediated PDT group (Fig. 7C). Furthermore, during the two-week observation period, mice in the five groups did not display any weight loss (Fig. 7B), indicating the good biocompatibility of cRGD-GQDs in vivo. After sacrificing the mice, H&E staining was employed to observe the microscopic changes in tumors and important organs of mice. As shown in Fig. 7D, tumors in the control group, cRGD-GQDs group, and irradiation group showed homogeneous cells with hyperchromatic nuclei and mitotic figures, while tumors in the GQD-mediated PDT group displayed slightly eosinophilic red staining. Furthermore, the cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT group showed obvious eosinophilic red staining with pyknotic nuclei and necrosis, whereby the necrotic body could also be observed. A plausible explanation for this phenomenon is that GQD-mediated PDT could induce cancer cell apoptosis in vivo, but tumors became resistant after repetitive therapy, resulting in the continuous growth of tumors. However, cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT could effectively inhibit tumors in vivo after repeated therapy, indicating that cRGD-GQDs could overcome the PDT resistance and facilitate a highly efficient anticancer therapy. Meanwhile, the important organs of mice showed neither pathological damage nor inflammation (Fig. S6), also indicating the good biocompatibility and minimal side effects of cRGD@GQD-mediated PDT.

Taken together, in the current study, we developed a polypeptide conjugated nanosystem, cRGD-GQDs, for highly efficient photodynamic therapy. The nanosystem could not only specifically target integrin αvβ3 positive cancer cells, but also overcame the PDT resistance mediated by free GQDs. The constructed cRGD-GQDs exhibited high light stability, singlet oxygen quantum yield and good biocompatibility, as well as an enhanced accumulation in integrin αvβ3 positive cancer cells, thus presenting an extraordinary antitumor ability. Furthermore, we found that nano-PS could induce PDT resistance by upregulation of Nrf2/ABCG2, Nrf2/HO-1 axis, and HIF-1α through the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway. However, it had little influence on cRGD-GQDs, which possessed the ability to evade PDT resistance pathway and as a result, cRGD-GQDs could efficiently accumulate in cancer cells and produce sufficient ROS for ablation of cancer cells during PDT. In summary, this work reported the PDT resistance mediated by nano-PS and provided an in-depth investigation of the PDT resistance mechanism for the first time. The synthesized nano-system (cRGD-GQDs) was demonstrated to be a promising modality for overcoming PDT resistance and targeting efficient anticancer therapy.
